textInfo.title: Elevated Monocytes (relative)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your blood test shows a slightly elevated percentage of monocytes, a type of white blood cell. This can be a sign of the body fighting off an infection, inflammation, or other conditions. Your absolute monocyte count is within the normal range.

textInfo.title: Cartilage defects in the inner part of the left knee
textInfo.medicalDescription: Im Kniegelenk Links im medialen Kompartiment tibialseitige Knorpeldefekte
textInfo.originalDescription: Im Kniegelenk Links im medialen Kompartiment tibialseitige Knorpeldefekte
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI of the left knee reveals defects in the cartilage on the tibial side (the top surface of your shinbone) of the medial compartment, which is the inner side of your knee joint. Cartilage is the smooth, slippery tissue that covers the ends of your bones, allowing them to glide over each other with little friction. A cartilage defect means there is a localized area where this smooth covering is damaged or worn away. This can be an early sign of osteoarthritis.

textInfo.title: High Total Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total cholesterol level is higher than the recommended range. High cholesterol can increase your risk of cardiovascular diseases, such as heart attack and stroke, by contributing to the buildup of plaque in your arteries. Your doctor may recommend lifestyle changes or medication to manage it.

textInfo.title: High LDL Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your LDL ('bad') cholesterol level is above the ideal range. High LDL cholesterol is a major risk factor for the buildup of fatty deposits (plaque) in arteries, which can lead to heart disease. Lifestyle modifications and sometimes medication are used to lower LDL levels.

textInfo.title: Elevated Transferrin Saturation
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your transferrin saturation level is higher than the recommended range. This measurement indicates how much of your iron-binding protein (transferrin) is being used to transport iron in the blood. Elevated levels, especially with high ferritin, may suggest iron overload, which could be related to diet, supplements, or genetic factors.

textInfo.title: Slightly High Left Parietal Lobe Gray Matter Volume
textInfo.medicalDescription: The normalized gray matter volume of the left parietal lobe (4.87%) is slightly above the normal range of [3.83 - 4.85]%.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The volume of gray matter in your left parietal lobe, when adjusted for total brain size, is slightly higher than the typical range for your age and gender. This is a minor finding and is generally not a cause for concern.

textInfo.title: Slightly Low Transferrin
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your transferrin level is slightly below the normal range. Transferrin is a protein that transports iron in the blood. A low level can be associated with high iron stores (as your high transferrin saturation suggests) or inflammation.

textInfo.title: Elevated Uric Acid
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your uric acid level is higher than the normal range. Uric acid is a waste product from the breakdown of purines, found in many foods. High levels can lead to the formation of crystals in your joints, causing gout, or in your kidneys, causing kidney stones. Lifestyle and dietary changes can often help lower uric acid levels.

textInfo.title: Elevated Total Bilirubin
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total bilirubin level is elevated. Bilirubin is a substance made when your body breaks down old red blood cells. While high levels can sometimes indicate liver or bile duct problems, a mild elevation can also be due to a harmless genetic condition called Gilbert's syndrome.

textInfo.title: Elevated LDL Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your LDL ('bad') cholesterol level is above the recommended limit. High LDL cholesterol contributes to the buildup of plaque in your arteries, which increases the risk of heart disease and stroke.

textInfo.title: Minor disc degeneration and bulging in the lower back
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI of the spine shows signs of disc degeneration with minor protrusion in your lower back, specifically affecting the discs between the second and fifth lumbar vertebrae (L2 to L5). The intervertebral discs act as cushions between the bones of your spine. Degeneration means these discs are losing some of their water content and height, which is a common part of aging. A protrusion is a slight bulge of the disc material, like a cushion that is being slightly squeezed, but it is not significantly pressing on the surrounding nerves in this case.

textInfo.title: Normal appearance of the hip joints
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI scan included an examination of your hip joints, and they were found to be normal. The radiologist did not see any signs of arthritis, cartilage damage, fluid collections, or other abnormalities in the bones or soft tissues of either of your hips.

textInfo.title: Minor cartilage cracks on the left kneecap
textInfo.medicalDescription:
Geringe
Knorpelfissuren an der Trochlea der Patella links
textInfo.originalDescription:
Geringe
Knorpelfissuren an der Trochlea der Patella links
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI of the left knee shows minor fissures, which are small cracks or clefts, in the cartilage on the trochlea of the patella. The patella is your kneecap, and the trochlea is the groove in the thighbone (femur) where the kneecap sits and glides. This finding indicates early-stage wear and tear of the cartilage on the back of your kneecap, a condition often referred to as chondromalacia patellae.

textInfo.title: Elevated GGT Level
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase (GGT) level, a liver enzyme, is slightly elevated. Elevated GGT can be a sign of liver or bile duct damage. It is often one of the first liver enzymes to rise and can be affected by alcohol consumption and certain medications.

textInfo.title: Elevated Total Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total cholesterol level is elevated. Cholesterol is a fatty substance necessary for building cells, but high levels can increase your risk of cardiovascular disease. It's important to consider this in conjunction with your other lipid levels, like LDL and HDL cholesterol.

textInfo.title: Elevated Monocyte Percentage
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your blood test shows a slightly higher than normal percentage of monocytes. Monocytes are a type of white blood cell that helps fight infection and remove damaged cells. A minor elevation is often temporary and can be caused by various factors, including infections or inflammation.

textInfo.title: Small kidney cysts on both sides
textInfo.medicalDescription: Einzelne kleine kortikale Nierenzysten bds. (maximal 10 mm rechts, Serie 55, Bild 72)
textInfo.originalDescription: Einzelne kleine kortikale Nierenzysten bds. (maximal 10 mm rechts, Serie 55, Bild 72)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a few small, simple cysts in the outer layer (cortex) of both of your kidneys. The largest of these cysts is on the right kidney and measures 10 mm. Kidney cysts are very common, fluid-filled sacs that are typically benign (non-cancerous) and usually do not cause any symptoms or affect kidney function. Their prevalence increases with age.

textInfo.title: Elevated Ferritin Level
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your ferritin level, which reflects your body's iron stores, is slightly above the normal range. High ferritin can be a sign of iron overload (hemochromatosis), inflammation, liver disease, or other conditions. Further evaluation may be needed to determine the cause.

textInfo.title: Elevated hs-CRP
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) level is 3.7 mg/L. This indicates a high level of inflammation in your body and is associated with an increased risk for cardiovascular events. Values above 3.0 mg/L suggest a high cardiovascular risk.

textInfo.title: High Total Bilirubin
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total bilirubin level is elevated. Bilirubin is a yellow substance made when your body breaks down old red blood cells. High levels can indicate liver problems, bile duct issues, or an increased rate of red blood cell destruction. A specific condition known as Gilbert's syndrome, which is harmless, can also cause mild elevation.

textInfo.title: High Uric Acid
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your uric acid level is elevated. High levels of uric acid in the blood can lead to the formation of crystals in your joints, causing a painful condition called gout. It can also be associated with kidney stones and an increased risk of kidney disease.

textInfo.title: Enlargement of the left seminal vesicle and normal prostate
textInfo.medicalDescription:
Sakkuläre Erweiterung der linken Samenblase (13 mm weit) mit Sedimentationsphänomen
(Serie 94, Bild 8). Ansonsten leichte Atrophie der Samenbläschen linksseitig. Prostata regelrecht.
textInfo.originalDescription:
Sakkuläre Erweiterung der linken Samenblase (13 mm weit) mit Sedimentationsphänomen
(Serie 94, Bild 8). Ansonsten leichte Atrophie der Samenbläschen linksseitig. Prostata regelrecht.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a sac-like enlargement of your left seminal vesicle, measuring 13 mm in width. The seminal vesicles are glands that produce fluid for semen. The imaging also shows a 'sedimentation phenomenon,' which means that denser fluid has settled at the bottom, a common finding when fluid is static. Additionally, there is a slight decrease in size (atrophy) of the seminal vesicles on the left side. Importantly, your prostate gland itself was examined and found to be normal in appearance.

textInfo.title: High Abdominal Subcutaneous Fat
textInfo.medicalDescription: The patient's abdominal subcutaneous adipose tissue (ASAT) volume is 15.8 L, which is above the reference range of 3.1 - 9.5 L. This value is in the 99.1st percentile.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your volume of abdominal subcutaneous adipose tissue (ASAT), which is the fat located just under the skin of your abdomen, is 15.8 L. This is above the normal range of 3.1 - 9.5 L. High levels of this type of fat can be associated with health risks.

textInfo.title: Fatty infiltration of the pancreas
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a finding called pancreatic lipomatosis. This means there is an increased amount of fat tissue within your pancreas. This is a common finding, often associated with aging or body weight, and is generally not considered a disease in itself. It simply describes the composition of the pancreatic tissue seen on the scan.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated Brain Volume Percentage
textInfo.medicalDescription: The normalized brain volume is 82.7%, which is slightly above the provided normal range of [73.2 - 81.7]%.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your brain volume, when measured as a percentage of your total skull volume, is 82.7%. This is slightly above the typical range of 73.2% to 81.7% for individuals of your age and gender. This is a subtle finding and its clinical significance should be discussed with your doctor.

textInfo.title: Elevated Total Bilirubin
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total bilirubin level is elevated. Bilirubin is a substance produced during the normal breakdown of red blood cells. Elevated levels can sometimes indicate liver or bile duct issues, but can also be due to a harmless genetic condition called Gilbert's syndrome.

textInfo.title: Small benign lesion (hemangioma) in a neck vertebra
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a small hemangioma in the sixth cervical vertebra (C6), which is one of the bones in your neck. A hemangioma is a common, non-cancerous (benign) finding composed of a small collection of blood vessels. These are very frequently found by chance on imaging studies and are generally considered a normal anatomical variant with no clinical significance.

textInfo.title: Elevated Uric Acid
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your uric acid level is elevated. High levels of uric acid can lead to the formation of crystals in your joints, causing a painful condition called gout. It can also be associated with kidney stones and other health issues.

textInfo.title: High hs-CRP (High Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) level is elevated, falling into the high-risk category for future cardiovascular events. hs-CRP is a marker of inflammation in the body, and persistent low-grade inflammation is linked to an increased risk of heart disease. This result suggests a higher-than-average risk.

textInfo.title: High Transferrin Saturation
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your transferrin saturation, which measures how much of the iron-binding protein in your blood is being used, is higher than normal. This, especially when seen with high ferritin, can suggest that your body has excess iron. It's often associated with conditions like hemochromatosis.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated Ferritin
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your ferritin level is slightly above the normal range. Ferritin is a protein that stores iron in your body. Elevated levels can be a sign of inflammation, liver conditions, or an iron overload disorder. However, a minor elevation like this is often not a cause for concern but should be monitored.

textInfo.title: Small benign lesion (hemangioma) in a mid-back vertebra
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a small hemangioma in the ninth thoracic vertebra (T9), which is one of the bones in your mid-back. A hemangioma is a common, non-cancerous (benign) finding that consists of a small tangle of blood vessels. These are typically discovered incidentally during imaging for other reasons and are not a cause for concern.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated Monocytes
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your blood test indicates a slightly elevated percentage of monocytes, a type of white blood cell involved in fighting infections and inflammation. This minor elevation is often temporary and can be related to the body's response to an infection or inflammation.

textInfo.title: Small pouches in the sigmoid colon (diverticula)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your scan shows a few diverticula in your sigmoid colon, which is the last part of the large intestine before the rectum. Diverticula are small, bulging pouches that form in the lining of the digestive system. The presence of these pouches is known as diverticulosis and is very common, especially in people over the age of 40. This finding is typically asymptomatic and does not cause problems unless the pouches become inflamed, a condition called diverticulitis.

textInfo.title: Slightly High Right Parietal Lobe Gray Matter Volume
textInfo.medicalDescription: The normalized gray matter volume of the right parietal lobe (4.76%) is slightly above the normal range of [3.81 - 4.72]%.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The volume of gray matter in your right parietal lobe, when adjusted for total brain size, is slightly higher than the typical range for your age and gender. This is a minor finding and is generally not a cause for concern.

textInfo.title: Low Transferrin Level
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your level of transferrin, a protein that transports iron in the blood, is slightly below the normal range. Low transferrin can sometimes be seen in conditions of iron overload, as the body tries to reduce iron absorption, or with certain liver conditions. This finding should be interpreted along with your other iron-related tests.

textInfo.title: Elevated LDL Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your LDL ('bad') cholesterol is elevated. High LDL levels contribute to the buildup of plaque in arteries, increasing the risk of heart disease and stroke. Lifestyle changes and, if necessary, medication can help lower this value.

textInfo.title: Elevated GGT
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase (GGT) level is elevated. GGT is a liver enzyme, and high levels can be a sign of liver or bile duct issues. It can also be elevated due to alcohol consumption or certain medications.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated Liver Fat
textInfo.medicalDescription: The mean liver fat fraction, measured via MRI in the specified region of interest (ROI), is 9.4%. This value suggests the presence of mild hepatic steatosis.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: An MRI measurement of the fat content in your liver showed a value of 9.4%. A healthy liver typically has less than 5% fat. This result indicates a mild accumulation of fat in your liver, a condition known as fatty liver.

textInfo.title: Elevated Transferrin Saturation
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your transferrin saturation is elevated. This measurement shows how much of your iron-binding protein (transferrin) is being used to transport iron. High levels, especially with high ferritin, can suggest iron overload.

textInfo.title: High Total Abdominal Fat
textInfo.medicalDescription: The patient's total abdominal fat volume is 21.4 L, which is significantly above the reference range of 8.94 - 10.1 L.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total abdominal fat volume is 21.4 L, which is significantly higher than the normal range of 8.94 - 10.1 L. Elevated total abdominal fat is a known risk factor for various metabolic conditions.

textInfo.title: Elevated Total Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total cholesterol level is higher than the recommended limit. Total cholesterol is a measure of all the cholesterol in your blood, including LDL ('bad') and HDL ('good') cholesterol. High levels can increase your risk of cardiovascular disease.

textInfo.title: Elevated Ferritin
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your ferritin level is slightly elevated. Ferritin is a protein that stores iron in your body, and high levels can indicate iron overload or inflammation. Given that your transferrin saturation is also high while transferrin is low, this may point towards an issue with iron metabolism.

textInfo.title: Elevated hs-CRP (High Cardiovascular Risk)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) level is 3.7 mg/L, which falls into the high-risk category (3.1 - 10.0 mg/L) for cardiovascular events. hs-CRP is a marker of inflammation in the body, and elevated levels are associated with an increased risk of heart disease.

textInfo.title: High Total Abdominal Adipose Tissue Index
textInfo.medicalDescription: The patient's total abdominal adipose tissue index is 6.4 L/m2, which is above the reference range of 1.5 - 5.2 L/m2. This value is in the 97.03rd percentile.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total abdominal adipose tissue index, which adjusts your abdominal fat volume for your body size, is 6.4 L/m². This is above the normal range of 1.5 - 5.2 L/m², indicating a disproportionately large amount of abdominal fat.

textInfo.title: Fatty Liver Disease (Steatosis Hepatis)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports a diagnosis of Steatosis Hepatis (ICD-10: K76.0), commonly known as fatty liver disease, which was diagnosed in March 2021.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Steatosis hepatis, commonly known as fatty liver disease, is a condition where excess fat builds up in the liver. It is often linked to factors like obesity, high cholesterol, or diabetes (non-alcoholic fatty liver disease), or excessive alcohol consumption. Management typically involves lifestyle changes such as diet modification, increased physical activity, and weight loss to prevent progression to more serious liver conditions.

textInfo.title: Elevated GGT
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase (GGT) level is elevated. GGT is a liver enzyme, and elevated levels can be a sign of liver or bile duct issues. It can also be raised by alcohol consumption or certain medications.

textInfo.title: Low Transferrin
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your level of transferrin, a protein that transports iron in the blood, is slightly low. This can sometimes be seen in conditions of iron overload, where the body tries to reduce iron transport.

textInfo.title: Elevated Non-HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your non-HDL cholesterol, which includes all 'bad' cholesterol types, is elevated. This is considered a strong indicator of cardiovascular risk. Managing this level is important for heart health.

textInfo.title: High Non-HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your non-HDL cholesterol, which includes all 'bad' types of cholesterol, is elevated. This is considered a strong indicator of cardiovascular risk, as it reflects the total amount of artery-clogging cholesterol in your blood. Managing this level is important for heart health.

textInfo.title: Elevated Non-HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Non-HDL cholesterol level is elevated. This value represents your total cholesterol minus the 'good' HDL cholesterol, and is considered a strong indicator of cardiovascular risk. An elevated level suggests a higher concentration of 'bad' cholesterol types in your blood.
